The New York City Economic Development Corporation determined that this issue is not within its jurisdiction because the helicopter did not belong to a tour company departing from a NYC heliport.Â  Instead, it was most likely a law enforcement or news gathering helicopter not subject to the routing protocols established for tour helicopters. It may also have been a helicopter that departed as a charter or tour from another City but flew over NYC airspace. Helicopter tours originating from outside NYC are not subject to the same City regulations as tours departing from heliports managed by NYCEDC. Any City regulation of these flights is pre-empted by federal law and subject only to the jurisdiction of the Federal Aviation Administration. descriptor: Branch or Limb Has Fallen Down
resolution_description: NYC Parks created a work order for the tree condition.  Please note that addressing this work can take up to a year or more depending on the higher priority work identified during this period and resources available to address this work.    NYC Parks work priority categories range from Category A through D, with A being the most critical and time-sensitive. We will complete all work in Category A first and address work in lower priority categories in accordance with available resources. For more information about the Tree Risk Management Program, visit the NYC Urban Forest page on the NYC Parks website at nyc.gov/parks/trees.   Alternatively, you may choose complete the work yourself by hiring an independent contractor. The contractor must complete a Tree Work Permit application on the NYC Parks website at nyc.gov/parks/trees.

NYC Parks inspected the tree and assigned it to priority category C. The agency works on trees based on priority. They categorize tree conditions from A (highest priority) to D (lowest priority).  There isnât a set timeframe for work in category C. If the condition gets worse, please file a new request. Parks will reinspect the tree and update the priority level if needed. You can track the status of the work through the Tree Work Hub at nyc.gov/parks/treework.  If you prefer not to wait, you can hire a qualified tree care contractor. You'd need to pay for the work yourself and get a Tree Work Permit. You can apply for the free permit at nyc.gov/parks/trees.  Are you interested in caring for street trees in your neighborhood? You can get trained and certified by Trees New York to prune low-hanging branches. To learn about the free Citizen Pruner program, visit treesny.org/citizen-pruners-stewardship.
